frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Ammi bhi wahi kehti hain, zamaana theek nahi hai toh ladki ko khud hi sambhal ke rehna padta hai. Itni raat ko akeli nikalogi ya random jagah jaogi toh phir problem ho jati hai, isme practical hona chahiye, emotional nahi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their personal responsibility and normalizes restrictions on their movement instead of questioning male violence. It encodes protective framing ('zamaana theek nahi hai') and victim-blaming through advice about not going out alone or late.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Zainab. Age 19. Single. A Muslim girl in a conservative family in Bhopal. You have been wearing the burqa since you were 15. You do not hate it. You do not love it. You have simply always worn it and never been given a space to form your own opinion about it. Today at college a professor gave a lecture about identity and clothing and asked students to write a short reflection on what they wear and why. You are sitting in the college bathroom during the break. You have your notebook open. You are trying to write the reflection. You cannot write it because every sentence you start about why you wear the burqa begins with what your father wants or what your community expects or what Allah has said according to your mother and you cannot find a sentence that begins with what you want. You are sitting with this for the first time.
